topic: Report of the Ombudsman, Children in care: complaints to the Ombudsman 2019–2023, February 2024
description: Wednesday, 16 July 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee Report of the Ombudsman, Children in care: complaints to the Ombudsman 2019–2023, February 2024 Report of the Ombudsman, OIA timeliness obligations: Compliance and practice in Kainga Ora Housing New Zealand Hearing of Evidence - John Allen, Chief Ombudsman - Zoey Henley, Assistant Ombudsman - Emma Leach, Senior Assistant Ombudsman - Ainslie Fenwick, Official Information Practice Investigations Manager
descriptionSummary: Report of the Ombudsman, Children in care: complaints to the Ombudsman 2019–2023, February 2024. Report of the Ombudsman, OIA timeliness obligations: Compliance and practice in Kainga Ora Housing New Zealand. John Allen, Chief Ombudsman

chunks.text: See, this came right here. Yeah. Yeah. Basically,

otherwise, Well, good morning and welcome to the Social Service and, uh, community Select Committee. Uh, thank you for coming in this morning. Uh, so look, uh, I wanna take an opportunity to, um, just for the purpose of those watching, uh, to introduce yourselves. Um, and then, uh, we'll, we'll go first to the, uh, report on Oman Children Care Complaints.

That was in 2019 to 2023. So Kia, and thank you for the opportunity to be here. Uh, my name's John Allen, and, uh, I'm three months or four months, uh, into a role as Chief Ombudsman. Uh, with me is Emma Leach, uh, on this side, uh, Emma is the Senior Assistant Ombudsman, uh, responsible for most things, including elements of, uh, the two reports, uh, that we are going to, uh, speak to.

And on this side is, so Zoe Heley, Zoe leads our Proactive Investigations team, uh, that actually did a lot of the, uh, the on the ground work, uh, that is reflected in these reports. Thank you very much. Well, perhaps if I just make a few very preliminary remarks, uh, in relation to these reports and in relation to the Children and Care report.

First, uh, clearly this was a report completed by my predecessor, uh, Peter Bocher, uh, and the t uh, and it describes challenges within Ari. Uh, the language used is confronted. Uh, the, then Chief Ombudsman said that OT needs to change at a scale, really required by a government agency, which is a fairly extraordinary sort of observation.

Uh, he said that, uh, the practice and process improvements that he had suggested in the report, uh, would make a material difference. But there was significant ground to be made up by the organization to rebuild trust and confidence, uh, in the communities, uh, which they serve.

He did identify some areas of good practice, uh, and you'll see that in the case studies, uh, that are included, uh, in the report. Uh, and he did recognize the importance, uh, of ot, uh, and the role that they play in our community with some of our most vulnerable, uh, children and whanau.

So it wasn't all bad news, uh, but it was certainly pretty confronting. Uh, the report focused on a range of areas. Uh, one was reports of concern. And reports of concern are obviously back in the papers at the moment, uh, with the inquest, uh, into Malachi Beck.

Uh, and in that context, failures to record reports of concern and adequate assessment of reports of concern, failure to consider multiple reports in a holistic manner. So those were significant. It considered uplifts and placements and some of the challenges that OT faced in that area in relation to inadequate search for suitable placements with whanau, failing to seek the views of tamari in terms of kotahi as well, in terms of where they might like to live and be located, uh, and how dealing with matters of urgency with, uh,

the non notified interim orders, uh, from the courts. So there was significant issues in that space. And another area that he particularly focused on, uh, was the disability community. Uh, and the challenges faced by parents and children with dis disability, uh, in the, uh, context of placement, uh, by ot.

And the findings specifically that he made, uh, in that regard, uh, was that no child and no parent, uh, should be disadvantaged as a consequence of disability in terms of the quick decisions, uh, as to placement, which was a position that OT have now adopted, uh, in their policies.

So those were the sorts of findings that this report, uh, identified. Uh, the case studies, I think, make for powerful reading, um, to identify how that actually plays out in the real lives, uh, of people, uh, in our communities. What I'm pleased to be able to tell the committee, uh, is that OT have taken this report extremely seriously, that OT have responded at an organizational level in terms of their practice and processes and the way in which they are operating, uh, their organization to address the challenges, uh,

which the former Chief Ombudsman, uh, raised. Uh, they have been engaging with our office on a regular basis to report on their progress. And we have been engaging in deep dives with the office, uh, on areas of particular importance, uh, and concern.

So I do believe that there is both the intention and some evidence of progress, uh, that these, this report will make a difference for OT and the important work that they do. That said, it's relatively early days. Uh, there are still complaints being received in our office in relation to elements of OTs work, which in itself is not that surprising, given the, the spread pressures, uh, and challenges with which, uh, the organization engages, but which is just evidence and a reminder, uh, that we need to keep our eye on this

and we need to keep progressing. So with those opening observations, Mr. Chairman, I'm happy to take any questions, uh, which I might have to ask my colleagues to, to answer given my relative newness, uh, in this space. Well, thank, thank you very much for that, uh, very good, uh, introduction to the topic. Um, so going to members, uh, who have, um, which needs all this, um, I, I'll start.

Um, um, look, I'm really interested in, in how you are going to, um, monitor the, the most effective way that you think you can monitor this and what is, what is clearly a very complex area, and you're dealing with humans with a lot of complexities, uh, both at the individual and, and the sort of broader community and family level.

How you gonna, how you, how you are going to ensure you can monitor the delivery of services within that complex human Yeah. Environment? Yeah, it, it is a challenge. Luckily, we are not alone in the task of Oversighting. Uh, Ari, uh, the Independent Children's Monitor, uh, is responsible for monitoring a lot of the outcomes that are being delivered.

And you might recall that they published a report relatively recently, uh, on the outcomes that were being delivered for Maori, uh, who of course comprise, uh, a significant component of the, uh, children and young people that are in care, uh, and are engaged with, um, ot.

So we have that group. Uh, and we also have the, uh, children's commissioner who acts as an advocate for children. Uh, so we have a sort of monitoring agency, uh, an advocacy agency, and ourselves, and I suppose we see ourselves as an investigative agency, uh, to be able to, the three of us work very closely together, uh, as we're required to do by the legislation.

Uh, and we engage, uh, you know, we share information, um, across our organizations in relation to ot. So I do think that that model is a, um, well-balanced, uh, monitoring, uh, capability, uh, for the government and for Parliament and for the people of New Zealand.

Uh, and I do believe on the basis of my short time, uh, that it is operating successfully, specifically to your question in terms of how we will respond. Uh, one, uh, we will continue to raise the profile of our office with people in care, uh, with the families involved, so that they know that they can, if they feel that things are not going right, uh, raise those matters with us as an independent body, uh, for investigation.

'cause I think that in many ways becomes the sort of tester, uh, often or the early sign o of things that might otherwise might actually be failing systemically, although we're only looking at it through a particular, uh, lens. Uh, the second thing we will do is we will continue our engagement with ot, uh, including the deep dive type engagements that we are undertaking, uh, because I do believe that they are having an effect in terms of both the culture and practice of that organization.

Thank you. Uh, question Menno, Morina, um, um, Reina, John, um, and, uh, and into your new role, albeit, um, um, you know, a few months, um, already, I'm pleased to hear the, your observations and, and, uh, just noting some of the remarks that you've made already.

Um, I like the sound of deep dive and regular reporting, those things. I think, um, that when the relationship works really well, people are prepared to just say even to, to reveal and to seek help. And so I'm, I'm appreciative of that. Um, um, John, what then it comes from me anyway, is what the capacity that's required even in your team, uh, to be able to, to, uh, continue this.

Because I don't think it's a, I mean, it, it gets to this point because people are pushed to complain. And, and so if the, if it never came to light, these things would still be just carrying on in, in the saddest way. So this is a good thing. I mean, it's, it's perverse, but it is a good thing. Uh, the truth is liberating, and I think it's a very good thing.

So yeah, just in terms of your team, your capacity, um, and that of your team to be able to keep, um, because the minute you deep dive, you know, then you've gotta go another 60 meters. That's how deep it goes. And so, um, and to, and to maintain the engagement and then build trust and confidence, because you also open those, sorry, Mr.

Chief, it's long call It All Bit. Just wanted to make those remarks and put those questions as openly as I can to you. Thank you. Thank you very much for the, uh, for the observations. Going to your first point, which is about the importance of illuminating things as a vehicle for addressing them. Uh, that is really what the role of this office is.

Uh, if you go to the legislation, the Ombudsman Act, the, uh, uh, the Official Information Act, the suite of other legislative frameworks that we operate under the, the founding principle is that by shining a light on things, uh, you identify challenges that can then be addressed, and you build trust and confidence for people, uh, in the organizations of the state, uh, and in their ability to engage in processes like parliamentary processes and lawmaking, uh, to be able to be, uh, more involved, uh, citizens in those, in those things.

And therefore, presumably trust the outcomes of those processes, uh, more than they might otherwise. So, uh, thank you for the recognition of the importance of those things. 'cause it is the foundation of the work that we do in terms of the, uh, capabilities of the organization and the capacity of the organization. Uh, we are lucky enough to have advice from a, uh, committee p manata, uh, which we have put together to advise, uh, the Chief Ombudsman, whoever that might be at a particular time.

Uh, and those people are drawn from, uh, senior ranks across Maori Dam, uh, and are people who have both lived experience and deep Mama, uh, in the community. Uh, I have found already in my time that the, the, the opportunity to engage with them, both in terms of, uh, relationship building, uh, with the, we Maori across, uh, uh, and in terms of some of the substance of the engagements and thinking about the issues that you raise and other issues that are impacting, um, has been extraordinarily helpful.

So that's one element of what we do. Uh, we are also growing our own capability, uh, within the organization in both tikanga, uh, and, and t Maori more generally, uh, to enable us to be able to support the Kopa as we move forward. And we have specific resourcing, um, which is engaged in the children in care space, uh, to ensure that we can continue to meet the growing, as you say, or well hopefully won't be growing.

But nonetheless, uh, the demands for investigation as we move forward. Can I just also add as well that we have, um, developed a training program for our staff that focuses on trauma-based care, understanding trauma, um, and we've also implemented a professional support system for our staff as well, so that they can receive professional supervision, um, for the work that they're doing and the impact that, that might have on them as well.

Thank you. And your, your comments are chief about sunlight. Reminds me of that Louis Brande quote. Uh, sunlight is the best as and victims. Yeah. Quite, uh, supplementary, I think. Oh, um, not, not, uh, primary. Primary. Okay. Thank you. Uh, will, Primary? Primary, yep. Um, Kilda, thank you very much for coming today.

Uh, I remember right back when we, um, came into this committee. We had the chief executive, um, responding some to some questions that I was asking around the description that the change level of change required was profound. And then in subsequent reports, the scale is referred to and the urgency.

I'm really concerned that we've seen significant cuts to orang TAM staffing and restructuring over 400 roles, cuts to community providers. And a damning auditor general's report about those decisions that are being made about cuts to community providers. And that the fact that the organization didn't know the impact that that would have on children, and then the most recent report that they've uploaded saying that there is a risk, they're not going to be able to meet their targets in terms of responding to the increased number of reports of concern.

So, while I'm heartened by some of the progress you're saying that they're making in terms of this other significant things are happening that I believe, and others are saying, uh, children will be harmed more as a result of these, uh, things and problems in the system. So, um, what are you doing about more recent, um, developments in that space?

Well, you'll have seen in the report, uh, that we are discussing this morning, uh, that, um, the former Chief Ombudsman laid down a widow challenge, uh, to the government, uh, to make sure that the organization was able to address the challenges, uh, that it faces.

Uh, it's not for me to comment on political judgments. Uh, that's not the role of the ombudsman. Uh, we are a pa an officer of parliament, and we try hard, hard to stay out of, uh, political matters. But I would say to you that I am heartened in my engagements with the chief exec, acting Chief executive of ot, um, over the period that I have been in this role by the clear priorities and focus, uh, that he has on the things that can most effectively shift the dial on the issues that you've been raising and which this report touches on.

So, uh, they do operate in a complex environment. I think we all operate in complex environments at the moment. Uh, my my view is that the recommendations that were made in the report by Mr. Boha are within that context, manageable for the organization, not withstanding, they're not on the, in themselves sufficient.

So Mr. Boha wasn't suggesting, I don't think that all of the, uh, operational changes he was suggesting would, while they would significantly advance the practice and improve the practice and performance of the organization, I don't think he would, I'm speaking for him, which is not a good idea. But nonetheless, I, I don't think he was suggesting that that was all that was needed to transform the performance of the organization, but it would be a significant step forward.

So I suppose it's in that spirit recognizing the wider challenges, recognizing the complexity, um, that I can say to you that I'm feeling more confident. Uh, that is because I'm seeing progress on the specific initiatives that Mr. Bocher outlined, and I do believe that they are making a positive impact on the performance of the organization, and I'm seeing focus from the chief executive, uh, and I believe that's appropriate given the challenges that he's trying to address with his colleagues.

Just one supplementary on that. Thank you for that, um, response. I suppose, um, given that urgency and what the result of, um, insufficient progress or change actually means in this context, which is children harmed, um, how we, how will you measure whether that earnest effort actually translates to meaningful change, um, in the outcomes?

So how, how will you, um, follow up on that as an organization or one of the, is it one of the other entity's responsibilities? Well, in terms of, uh, reports of concern, for example, uh, we are confident that there has been progress made on reports of concern, particularly in terms of the issue of multiple complaints not being treated in a holistic way.

Uh, so with the new technologies that they are deploying, uh, there is the opportunity for that information to be shared more easily and more fully as I understand it, uh, across the, uh, the organization. So I do think that there is some progress being made in terms of, I can, if I could, so using that example, um, the technology allows it to happen.

How will you check whether that is in fact happening? Yeah, so we have, we have a number of mechanisms that we can use. One is that we're in, in touch with them, as I say, to monitor performance against recommendations that were made. And they are continuing to report to us as recently as March, I think it was the most recent report, um, on that progress.

So we will continue to see that progress and to comment on that progress. We will continue with these deep dives that I've talked about. Uh, and we have the ability, uh, through own motion, um, investigation, so we don't have to wait for a complaint. Mm. If we have the view that there is something that needs to be looked at again, uh, then we can do that, uh, on our own initiative and we will. Mm.

Thank you. Thank you so much. And thank you for the sensitivity that your and these matters and Talking with. And so I just wanna talk about young people and the importance of their voice. Yeah, Yeah. We, um, part of the Royal Commission of Inquiry recommendations was that there was an independent agency that advocated for children and children's voice and care, and that funding has actually stopped, um, in this last, um, budget round.

So I am interested to know how that is going and how these children are going to have an independent advocate to support their voice to be heard. Well, um, one, this report clearly identifies the importance of that voice being heard, uh, and clearly challenges OT to shift its practices to ensure that that voice is heard, uh, in a variety of different situations.

So, so the report certainly supports the concern and ambition, uh, that you have described. Uh, the office of the Children's Commissioner, uh, is intended in my understanding of that office, uh, to be an advocate for children, um, not just in care, of course, but, but, uh, children generally.

But I know that for the, uh, current, uh, leader of that team, uh, that the children and care component is a particularly important component that she has been, um, very focused on. So I do think there is a, uh, a, a specialist advocacy agency that is engaging more effectively or, or, or is engaging reasonably effectively in that way.

Uh, the last point that I would make is that we are working hard to raise the profile of our office, uh, with young people. And again, you'll see that reflected in the report, uh, from Mr. Nacha. Uh, the word ombudsman isn't one that sort of resonates necessarily with, uh, kotahi, uh, or Tamari.

Uh, and, uh, you know, Scandinavian origins can be a bit opaque at times to, uh, to people here in, but, but, uh, what we are trying to do is get the message out that, that we are here, uh, we listen, uh, and we are able to act, and we are independent, and we are confidential, and we are non, uh, adversarial.

We're an inquisitorial, uh, process. Uh, all of those things, I think hopefully can enable young people as they get to understand what we might be able to do for them, uh, to engage with us more effectively. Thank, thank you very much, SHA. That is the time we have available for that piece of work. We, we will now shift to the next one, which we also have with you, which is the report of the Osman RIA, timeliness obligations, compliance and Practice, buying an Order, housing New Zealand.

Thank you Zoe, Mr. Um, here with us for that as well. Sorry, Zoe. Alright. Um, as a look, if if maybe the same format, it would be helpful, um, in your hands. Again. Uh, thank you Kiana. Uh, and, um, I've been joined Ainsley, no, no, Ainsley.

Um, so, uh, so Ainsley on this side and Ainsley did actually lead the work that's, uh, that's, uh, in this report. So, so just, just contextually, uh, this report was a response to what was being presented in the media and what Mr. Boha was then concerned about, which were criticisms of the official Information Act as becoming effective, ineffective from the media point of view, uh, in their ability to, um, uh, to, uh, get the information they needed to be able to tell the stories that need to be told to our people,

to engage them in political and other processes, uh, so that he wanted to test, uh, whether or not that was the case. And he investigated a range of organizations, as you will see, one of which was Kai Aura. Uh, and in those investigations, he was asking himself the question, uh, is there activity within these organizations which is trying effectively to use this legislation to defeat in some way the objects, uh, of the legislation, uh, in the context of Kura?

Uh, the answer was very clearly, no, there was nothing of that kind. In fact, he said, no, there is no evidence of Kura misusing, uh, the OIA, uh, and as you will have seen, uh, in the report, uh, that he that has, was prepared, uh, there was actually some examples of good practice, uh, within that agency, uh, which he was encouraged by.

There were a few areas that he did focus on. Uh, he focused on the question of engagement with ministers, which is always a, a complicated, uh, question because obviously the, um, uh, cabinet manual requires ministers to be able to operate on a no surprises basis.

So there is a need for agencies to, uh, notify, uh, ministers in relation to, um, oia. Uh, but, uh, it's important to recognize that the decision in relation to, uh, those, uh, process out of those processes still risks with the ce, uh, of the agency.

And those sorts of engagements should be able to be managed within the, uh, up to 20 days. Uh, that's, uh, that's prescribed, uh, within the legislation. So there was a bit of a discussion about that, and I'm happy to talk about that. There was, the use of extensions, uh, was a particular issue here, because at that time, about 50%, I think it was, of the, um, oi requests that were going to Korra were being extended, and they had a sort of mandatory period, a sort of default period.

They were extending them for about 20 days, just sort of as a, as a sort of default. Uh, and so, uh, you'll have seen that the, uh, that, uh, Mr. Bocher, uh, was critical of that approach, uh, both as being inappropriate, uh, in sort of commonsensical terms, but also inappropriate in law, uh, and, uh, was made it very clear that you can only extend for legitimate purposes and you can only extend for a reasonable period.

And there shouldn't be a sort of blanket approach to, uh, to how that, um, how that was done. Uh, and then, uh, what else, what other areas did he have? Oh, the other area that he talked about was, was the question of communicating a decision before the process of reaching the decision had been, um, had been finalized.

Uh, and that, that's a concern because it potentially can, could be seen as, as, uh, playing to the, uh, getting a completed result even though it wasn't completed. And even though the information then wasn't, uh, hadn't yet been provided. So you'll see the analysis of that.

So those were the sort, those were the three sorts of areas, but as I say, there was some good, uh, there was some good practice. And the, and, and I think the positive news again, uh, is that ka Laura took this seriously and have acted on it. And just, just on the question of extensions, so they had about 50% extensions in the report, you'll have seen that had gone down to about 25% by the time the report was completed.

It's now gone down to 12%. So, so they have changed their processes and they are seeing outcomes as a consequence. Well, thank you very much. Um, I'm gonna take the opportunity to ask one question. Cool. It's something that's, that's been of interest for me for a number of years. I have to confess that it's the, um, there, there's a 20 days that specifies in legislation for IA requests to be responding to.

Um, but, um, from, I'm just have a memory, I think it's without undue delay, and it seems a number of agencies seem to take the 20 days as a deadline as opposed to a maximum. Yeah. I just wonder if you have any comments on that. You Are right. Agencies do take, uh, 20 days as a, as a, uh, deadline and not a maximum.

Uh, and they're wrong. It's a, it's, it's, it's not what the law requires. Uh, and so, uh, I, I think just about every report I've read since I've been in this office prepared by Mr. Bocher, um, commented on this, uh, and commented on the, uh, impropriety of this, uh, and commented on the need for, uh, agencies to, uh, get there as quickly as, as practicable.

It's interesting when you read the Public Service Commission, um, data, uh, that comes out, so that, that's the core public service. So it's not, it's not everything, but nonetheless, the core public service. And you may have seen the data that came out on the 13th of March. They, um, they said that the average time to respond to an OIA request, um, in that report, uh, was 13 days.

So that does suggest that there are agencies that are doing it well, uh, and are getting onto it, uh, and, um, being, uh, timely about it. But your, your point is well made, uh, and it deserves repeating every time you, uh, uh, we appear in front of you. So thank you, Mr. Chairman. Let's, let's keep getting that message out there, Helen.

Um, I've got two. I have to pick one. Um, the first thing is, thanks, it's great report. And I wanted to know, um, I could see that it had had impact in that organization in terms of just picking up on the extensions. Um, it is clearly just an example. You've sort of gone on, is that right? You've gone into one, but it could be any agency.

Um, is there, uh, is there a satisfaction that this is true of all agencies or are you going to, is your plan to do, um, this like an audit every now and then on an organization so everybody picks up good habits. Um, and then the second part of that question is, what about the quality of the answers?

Did you look, 'cause that must be quite a hard thing to audit people sidestepping and answering the question, but not answering the question. Is there any capacity to look at that? Because I would say that's your more risky area. I'll ask, I'll ask the investigator to like, to answer the, the, the second part of your question in terms of the first part of your question, this report was one of seven agencies that we investigated.

Yes. In this particular exercise. Um, I've just, uh, uh, completed signing up. I think we've completed, haven't we? All the, uh, about six local government agencies that we've done relatively recently. So the answer is yes. You're keeping up rolling it Out. We move around. Rolling. Good. It does, it's a whole team. Yeah.

And we've made a commitment to undertake regular practice reviews every year. And so that includes central and Local government. Fantastic. Yeah. Correct. Now in terms Of, So the, the second part of your question, so, um, the actual decision, that's the role of the complaints function of the office. So the practice investigations won't really look at the actual decision of an OAA request. What we do do is look at how that OAA request was processed.

And we do that by looking at, um, a file review of, of particular files. But we also look and at, uh, a pillar of what these reports look at is performance monitoring. And within that performance monitoring, we are looking at the agencies wanting to do, uh, we encourage them to do their own quality assurance. So you'll see in a number of the reports, we will be, uh, if they don't do that quality assurance themselves to start doing that quality assurance to pick, um, a sample of OAA responses in a particular period and go through that process themselves

and make, you know, assure themselves that they've answered the question. Is it was context provided, was, um, you know, grammar. Okay. And was it clear? So that's, that's how we kind of cover those two angles. I hope I've answered question. Okay. I think so, yeah. I, I think the, the two other points, um, we often use, uh, surveys, uh, to go out to the public or to the organization that people that work to deal with the organization to get a sense of how they feel, uh, that things are going.

We often interview the staff, um, of the organization or survey the staff of the organization as to what they think the attitude of their leadership. And again, you'll see that in the, uh, report. Yeah, the culture, uh, the attitude of the leadership is towards, uh, towards openness. 'cause that's a pretty good indicator of, uh, of how things are going.

Uh, so, so there are a whole range of techniques that are being used. Have you ever, sorry, is that alright? Sorry. Um, have you ever done something where you've compared two sources for the same information? So I Have heard That if I ask the police in one area and I ask them in another for the same information, I'll get a different timeline and a different exposure of information. Yeah.

Yeah. So, so we try, what we do is we look at is, uh, John mentioned we get our information from a range of areas and the larger the, um, organization and depending on how they set up their OAA function, um, we will look at the OAA responses from various branches to be able to compare.

But, um, with kind Aura, they operated a mix model. So, you know, we were looking at that specifically within Korra. So, uh, but we get our information from a number of sources, staff survey. We speak to a large amount of staff within an agency. The C chief executive, um, is also spoken to, so in the public survey.

So we get a lot of information and we also do those far reviews and read all the policies and just to Check that you are seeing if it's manipulative. Absolutely. Yeah. Thank you. Um, if I, well, if it's written down, if, uh, so, so the record keeping, which is also a big part of what we look at and you'll see in this report and the number of, uh, pretty much all our reports.

We also comment on, uh, information management and record keeping. 'cause if you don't have the record, it's uh, you, you know, record keeping and an efficient OAA per, uh, process go hand in hand. And You've criticized them for that in this report, haven't you? We did, yeah. Thank you. Yeah, I, I've actually, If there are gaps in the record, we, it's hard for us to yeah.

To understand what went on, uh, in the decision making process. Uh, so we, we pick that up if it's, thank you. If there are gaps. Thank you for that. Yeah. I, I actually have a couple supplemental Off the back of that, just the information management Side of it. Yeah. Um, and ba basically information creation side of it as well. I mean, it obviously varies across organizations in terms of how good their internal systems are for collecting information and storing information.

Any comments on that and and respect of this report? Well, there, there were, uh, as, as we've just said because, uh, we found practice that wasn't, um, you know, up to scratch in terms of the, of the record keeping. Uh, again, there seems to have been progress in that space, if I understand it correctly, uh, with the, uh, with automation of their, some of their processes and, and documentation storage mechanisms that they now, that they're now utilizing.

So I think that is being addressed. We do find this commonly, uh, in organizations where, uh, the material that is being used to frame a decision is either not adequately described, uh, in the document or, or the document is not adequately stored, uh, in a manner that enables it to be retrievable.
